tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.workaround:
- Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * Please look into your compiler manual for march=native or equivalent
 - Use vector aligned instructions:
  1) align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries
  2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: read your compiler manual.
<<image_vec_align>>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 1 suboptimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt:
3 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
7 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 22 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 15: addition or subtraction (5 inside FMA instructions)
 - 7: multiply (5 inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 176 bytes (22 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 28 bytes (3 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.11 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 24.00 cycles. At this rate:
 - 11% of peak load performance is reached (7.33 out of 64.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 3% of peak store performance is reached (1.17 out of 32.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 24.00 to 13.50 cycles (1.78x speedup).

header: 5% of peak computational performance is used (0.92 out of 16.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 24.00 to 13.50 cycles (1.78x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
  * If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly
  * If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA)
gain.details:
33% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 14% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 25% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
 - 41% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 29% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 24.00 to 4.28 cycles (5.61x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.01x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 5 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

hint.workaround:
- Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * Please look into your compiler manual for march=native or equivalent
 - Use vector aligned instructions:
  1) align your arrays on 32 bytes boundaries
  2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: read your compiler manual.
<<image_vec_align>>

header:
Warnings:
 - Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
 - Ignoring paths for analysis
 - Too many paths. Rerun with max-paths=16
 - RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
 - Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option.
This loop has 16 execution paths.
The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck.
Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
 - hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
 - turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX
